nativeRange.habitat: Smaller tributaries, backwaters, oxbows, and marginal lakes with slow-moving or still water; often near dense vegetation and over soft, muddy substrate.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential for barbel protection)
driftwood and leaf litter for cover
smooth rounded substrate where sand not available

diet.notes: The Emerald Cory is a foraging omnivore that sifts through the substrate in search of food. In the wild it feeds on insect larvae, worms, and small crustaceans. In the aquarium it readily accepts sinking pellets, algae wafers, and frozen or live foods such as bloodworm (chironomid larvae), Tubifex, daphnia, and brine shrimp. It should receive targeted bottom-feeder feeding rather than being expected to survive on leftovers that drift down from fish feeding higher in the water column.

avoidWith: fish small enough to be startled by their size
boisterous fast-moving species that compete for bottom food

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by NonstickRon, 2592x1944. Sharp side-on shot of a live adult showing the diagnostic brilliant green-blue metallic iridescence along the deep flank, enlarged dorsal fin and barbels; best colour and resolution of the set.
Photo by Maschinenkanone, 994x720. Whole live adult in a planted tank with clear green metallic flank sheen and visible barbels; correct ID, sharp, but lower resolution than the recommended pick.

Photo by Paul (London Zoo), 1600x1067. Large shoal of emerald corys foraging over sand, representing the species' strong schooling behaviour; slightly soft/hazy through tank glass so kept as a non-recommended group shot.

summary: The Emerald Cory (Brochis splendens) is an armored catfish from the Amazon basin, recognized by its striking green-blue metallic iridescence. Slightly larger than most Corydoras, it reaches around 7–8 cm and is best kept in groups of six or more in a well-planted aquarium with a fine sand substrate.
careNotes:
The Emerald Cory's current accepted scientific name is Brochis splendens (Castelnau, 1855). A 2003 taxonomic revision merged the genus Brochis into Corydoras, placing it under the name Corydoras splendens, which still appears on most retail labels and older care guides. A subsequent 2024 revision restored Brochis as a valid separate genus. Additional historical names include Callichthys splendens (the original description), Brochis dipterus, and Corydoras semiscutatus.

The species is recognized by its brilliant green-blue metallic iridescence along the flanks. Juveniles are sometimes sold under the trade name 'hi-fin cory' because of their noticeably enlarged dorsal fin, which is proportionally smaller in adults.

Fine sand is the preferred substrate — coarse gravel damages the sensitive barbels (the short whisker-like sensory organs around the mouth) that the fish depends on for foraging. Groups of at least 6–8 are recommended; the Emerald Cory tends to show a stronger schooling drive than many other corys and generally becomes more active and confident in larger numbers. A tank with a 120 cm footprint is the practical minimum for a proper school. Hardness tolerance is wide (approximately 36–268 ppm, or gH 2–15), making the species adaptable to most community water conditions. Like all members of the Callichthyidae family, the Emerald Cory is a facultative air-breather — it uses a vascularized portion of its intestine to absorb oxygen directly from the air — and will periodically dart to the surface. This is normal behavior and not a sign of oxygen deficiency, though good aeration is still recommended. Provide shaded areas and driftwood cover; the species is not strictly nocturnal but tends to appreciate low-light retreats during the day.
breedingNotes: Breeding is possible in captivity with some conditioning. Separating a group of well-fed adults into a dedicated breeding tank with slightly cooler, soft water and strong water movement is generally recommended to simulate the seasonal rainfall triggers that prompt spawning in the wild. Females are noticeably rounder and heavier-bodied than males. Spawning follows the typical Corydoras-style T-position, in which the male fertilizes eggs as the female holds them cupped in her pelvic fins. Eggs are deposited near the water surface on broad plant leaves or the aquarium glass and can number in the hundreds to over 1,000 per spawn, with clutches of 830–1,100 eggs reported. Removing the eggs to a separate raising vessel with gentle aeration is advisable to prevent fungal infection. Fry are relatively large and can accept microworms and baby brine shrimp soon after becoming free-swimming.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras splendens (Brochis splendens)
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided size (70–80 mm SL), temperature (20–28°C), pH (5.0–8.0), hardness (36–268 ppm), tank dimensions (120×45 cm minimum), diet, behavior, native range (Amazon basin; Brazil, Peru, Ecuador, Colombia), substrate requirements, and synonymy (formerly Brochis splendens; historical synonyms include Callichthys splendens, Brochis dipterus, Corydoras semiscutatus). Note: SeriouslyFish lists the accepted name as Corydoras splendens following Britto 2003, but 2024 Dias/Britto revision restores Brochis — this entry uses Brochis splendens as the current accepted name per the task brief.

additional.name: FishBase — Brochis splendens / Corydoras splendens
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 9.9 cm SL, 40 g), temperature (22–28°C), pH (5.8–8.0), native range (Amazon River basin, South America), habitat (sluggish freshwater with dense vegetation), diet (insect larvae, worms, small crustaceans), taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes, subfamily Corydoradinae), egg count (830–1,100). FishBase carries the species under both Brochis splendens and the synonym Corydoras splendens.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Care Guide
additional.notes: General Corydoras guide; confirms Emerald Cory as one of the most popular trade varieties. Notes group size minimum of 6, smooth sand/gravel substrate, temperature 72–82°F (22–28°C), pH 6.5–7.8, tank minimum 20 gallons (76 L) for typical Corydoras but larger recommended for the bigger Brochis/Emerald type. Non-picky feeding behavior confirmed.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Brochis splendens (archived profile)
additional.notes: Earlier SeriouslyFish profile under former genus name; consistent data. Confirmed facultative air-breathing via vascularized intestine, juvenile 'hi-fin cory' trade name, 10–12 dorsal-fin rays as distinguishing feature from Brochis britskii (15–18 rays), and group size recommendation of 6–8 individuals minimum.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding is considered moderately difficult. It requires conditioning adults with varied live and frozen foods, followed by a seasonal trigger — typically a significant water change with slightly cooler water to simulate the onset of the rainy season. Eggs must be removed after spawning to prevent predation and fungal loss. Clutches of 830–1,100 eggs have been reported. Fry are large enough to accept microworms and baby brine shrimp shortly after becoming free-swimming.
